content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

August 21, 1990

Karen Bass
Accountant
Charles E. Bray, CPA
Business and Financial Consultant
2626 Nicholson Street
San Leandro, CA 94577

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-478

Dear Ms. Bass:

This is in response to your letter requesting information
regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1
Since there is no specific governmental decision on which you seek
advice, we are treating your letter as a request for informal as-
sistance.2

QUESTION

Is there any requirement under the Act to segregate campaign
funds received prior to January 1, 1989?

CONCLUSION

There is no requirement for a candidate to segregate campaign
funds received prior to January 1, 1989. Section 85306 of the Act
has been ruled unconstitutional.

FACTS

Your letter does not include the specific circumstances which
gave rise to your question. You do indicate that your firm is

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916)322-5660

File No. I-90-478
Page 2

responsible for accounting of an account containing campaign funds
collected prior to January 1, 1989.

DISCUSSION

The provisions of Proposition 73, an initiative enacted in
1989, included Section 85306, which permitted any person who pos-
sessed campaign funds on January 1, 1989 to expend those funds for
any lawful purpose other than to support or oppose a candidacy for
public office. As a result of the enactment of Section 85306,
candidates and incumbents were instructed to segregate pre-1989
funds from those subsequently collected.

However, as a result of litigation pertaining to Section
85306,³ there has been a court ruling that Section 85306 is
unconstitutional. Therefore, there is no difference in permitted
usage between funds collected prior to January 1, 1989 and funds
received after that date, and there is no reason to segregate the
earlier funds.

Bear in mind, however, that other provisions of Proposition
73 which deal with use of campaign funds remain in full force and
effect. For example, a candidate may not, under Section 85304,
transfer his or her campaign funds to another candidate.
Transfers of funds between a candidate's own committees are pres-
ently permitted under a preliminary injunction handed down in the
SEIU case. We are presently awaiting for the court's final ruling
in the SEIU case, which has challenged several provisions of
Proposition 73.

I trust the above answers your question on segregation of
pre-1989 campaign funds. If you need further information, please
do not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Susan L. Borrow
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:SLB:plh


³ Service Employees International Union, et al. v. FPPC, Case No.
CIVS 89-0433 LKK-JFM, U.S. District Court, Eastern District of
California ("SEIU").

As a result of the enactment of Section 85306, candidates and incumbents were instructed to segregate pre-1989 funds from those subsequently collected.

However, as a result of litigation pertaining to Section 85306, there has been a court ruling that Section 85306 is unconstitutional. Therefore, there is no difference in permitted usage between funds collected prior to January 1, 1989 and funds received after that date, and there is no reason to segregate the earlier funds.

Bear in mind, however, that other provisions of Proposition 73 which deal with use of campaign funds remain in full force and effect. Transfers of funds between a candidate's own committees are presently permitted under a preliminary injunction handed down in the SEIU case. I trust the above answers your question on segregation of pre-1989 campaign funds. If you need further information, please do not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.
